label: Uneasy heat
summary: A heavy day dominated by a deadly European heat dome, even as the Gulf kept thawing and the day's ledger held real bright spots. Western Europe's 'omega block' heatwave intensified: France recorded its hottest day since records began in 1947, with 44.3C in Pissos and roughly 45C forecast across the south, while the running drowning toll of people trying to escape the heat climbed toward 48 and at least 18 died of heat-related causes, including two children left in a car. The heat broke the grid as well as records, knocking out power to as many as 106,000 homes in Brittany's Finistère after a transformer failure, shuttering hundreds of schools, and cutting hours at the Louvre and Eiffel Tower; Britain logged its hottest June day at 36.1C. Africa again carried the gravest weight: the Bundibugyo Ebola epidemic in DR Congo rose to 1,094 confirmed cases and 277 deaths as the UN warned the outbreak is outpacing the response, and Sudan's war left nearly 19.5 million people in acute food insecurity with famine risk spreading in Darfur. The Iran war's end remained tantalisingly close but unfinished: the IAEA chief said inspectors would visit bombed nuclear sites under the interim deal, but Tehran insisted no such visits are planned before a final agreement, and the U.S. Senate passed a historic war-powers resolution to restrain further strikes. Against the gloom, oil fell below $70 a barrel to its lowest since February, easing cost-of-living pressure worldwide; Argentina's Messi became men's World Cup all-time top scorer; archaeologists unveiled a vast Viking textile complex in Denmark; South Asia's vultures kept rebounding; U.S. deaths of despair fell; and labs reported quantum and PFAS-breakdown advances. Cheaper energy and a holding ceasefire are real, but a lethal heatwave, a worsening outbreak and a deepening famine pin the mood just below the neutral line (headline nudged -1 for the worsening climate emergency).

drivers.headline: France records its hottest day since measurements began in 1947, hitting 44.3C in Pissos with up to 45C across the south, as the running toll of people drowning while trying to escape the heatwave climbs toward 48 and at least 18 die of heat-related causes, including two children left in a car

A heat-driven transformer failure cuts power to as many as 106,000 French homes in Finistere as Europe's record 'omega block' heat dome strains the grid, with priority restoration given to hospitals and nursing homes and generators trucked to care facilities

The Bundibugyo Ebola epidemic in DR Congo rises to 1,094 confirmed cases and 277 deaths with neighbouring Uganda reporting 20 cases, as the UN warns the outbreak continues to outpace the response and nearly 80 health workers have been infected, with no licensed vaccine for the strain

UN agencies warn nearly 19.5 million people in Sudan now face acute food insecurity with about 135,000 in catastrophic conditions across 14 Darfur and Kordofan hotspots, as the lean season deepens famine risk and only a fifth of the 2026 aid plan is funded

A dispute clouds the war-ending U.S.-Iran deal as the IAEA chief says inspectors will visit Iran's bombed nuclear sites under the interim accord while Tehran's foreign ministry insists there are no plans for any such visits before a final agreement is reached

The U.S. Senate passes a war-powers resolution 50-48 directing President Trump to halt military action against Iran without congressional approval, the first time both chambers have invoked the War Powers Act to pull forces from a conflict, even as the measure is seen as largely symbolic

Crude oil falls below $70 a barrel to its lowest level since late February, with Brent down about 4% near $73, as rising tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz and progress in U.S.-Iran peace talks ease supply fears and take pressure off cost of living worldwide
Wall Street drifts as the S&P 500 closes at 7,358, down 0.1%, and the Nasdaq slips 0.4% on renewed AI-stock jitters ahead of Micron earnings, while the Dow edges up 0.3% in a cautious, mixed session

Lionel Messi scores twice to become the all-time leading goalscorer in men's World Cup history as Argentina advance to the knockout rounds, a unifying sporting milestone celebrated across South America

Archaeologists unveil a sprawling 100,000-square-metre Viking Age textile production site at Soften near Aarhus, Denmark, with more than 80 pit houses, spindle whorls, looms, silver coins and glass beads underscoring the sophistication of Viking society more than 1,000 years ago

South Asia's vultures stage a remarkable recovery hailed as one of the great conservation success stories, with the once-99.9%-collapsed white-rumped vulture rebounding after India, Nepal and Pakistan banned the drug diclofenac and ran breeding programmes

Labs report fresh science gains as researchers show intense-UV hydrogen radicals can break down stubborn PFAS 'forever chemicals' without added chemicals, RIKEN proposes one-way quantum synchronisation of phonons, and U.S. deaths of despair keep falling with drug deaths down 26% in 2024

sources_consulted: Wikipedia (2026 European heatwaves / 2026 Ebola epidemic / 2026 Labour Party leadership crisis / 2026 Israel-Lebanon ceasefire / Risk of Famine in Sudan)
